meta.title: Dinosauria Phylogeny
meta.description: Phylogenetic tree of Dinosauria and related archosaurs
sources: Nesbitt, S.J. (2011) The early evolution of archosaurs
Baron, M.G. et al. (2017) A new hypothesis of dinosaur relationships

info.description: The crocodilian lineage of archosaurs, including modern crocodilians and many extinct forms.
Large rauisuchid predator of the Late Triassic, up to 4m long. Walked on four legs but may have reared on two.
Includes modern crocodilians and their closest extinct relatives.
Giant crocodilian that reached 10-12 metres. Preyed on dinosaurs at the water's edge.
Enormous crocodyliform from Early Cretaceous Africa, nicknamed 'SuperCroc'.
The bird-line archosaurs, including pterosaurs, dinosaurs, and birds.
Flying reptiles, the first vertebrates to evolve powered flight.
Early Jurassic pterosaur with a disproportionately large head and two distinct tooth types.
Long-tailed pterosaur from the Late Jurassic.
The first pterosaur ever identified. Small, with a wingspan of about 1.5 metres.
Large, toothless pterosaurs of the Late Cretaceous.
Large toothless pterosaur with a wingspan up to 7 metres.
Giant pterosaurs including the largest flying animals ever known.
One of the largest known flying animals, with a wingspan of 10-11 metres.
Enormous azhdarchid from Romania. Apex predator on the Late Cretaceous island of Hateg.
Tiny dinosauromorph from Argentina, about 40cm long. Close to the ancestry of dinosaurs.
Small, cursorial archosaurs closely related to dinosaurs.
Early dinosauriforms, possibly herbivorous or omnivorous.
The dinosaurs: one of the most successful groups of animals to have ever lived.
Lizard-hipped dinosaurs, including theropods and sauropodomorphs.
Among the earliest dinosaurs, predatory and bipedal.
One of the oldest known dinosaurs, from Argentina.
Tiny early dinosaur from Argentina, only 1 metre long. Its classification is debated — possibly a basal sauropodomorph.
Bipedal, mostly carnivorous dinosaurs. Includes the ancestors of birds.
Small, agile predator from the Late Triassic. Hundreds found together at Ghost Ranch, New Mexico.
Early Jurassic theropod with distinctive paired crests on its skull. No evidence of venom or a frill.
Diverse group of theropods, particularly successful in the southern continents.
Large predator with a horn on its snout.
Major predators of Gondwana, characterised by short snouts and tiny arms.
Fast predator with bull-like horns and tiny arms.
Apex predator of Late Cretaceous Madagascar. Evidence suggests it was cannibalistic.
Stiff-tailed theropods. The most diverse clade of predatory dinosaurs.
The first dinosaur to be formally named and described (1824).
Semi-aquatic theropods with elongated crocodile-like snouts.
Possibly the largest carnivorous dinosaur. Semi-aquatic with a sail on its back.
Fish-eating theropod with a large claw on each hand. Discovered in Surrey, England.
Large spinosaurid from Niger with a low sail on its back. Name means 'crocodile mimic'.
Large-bodied predatory theropods including allosaurs and carcharodontosaurs.
Apex predator of the Late Jurassic Morrison Formation.
Giant predators named for their shark-like teeth. Rivals to tyrannosaurs in size.
Enormous predator from North Africa with a skull up to 1.6m long.
One of the largest known terrestrial carnivores, from Patagonia.
Large predator from Early Cretaceous North America with tall neural spines along its back.
Feathered theropods, from tiny insectivores to giant tyrannosaurs.
Small, gracile coelurosaurs. Among the first dinosaurs found with preserved soft tissue impressions.
One of the smallest known non-avian dinosaurs, about the size of a chicken.
First dinosaur found with preserved feathers (filamentous integument). Reddish-brown and white banded tail.
The tyrannosaur lineage, from small early forms to the giant apex predators of the Late Cretaceous.
Small feathered tyrannosaur from China. Shows the lineage started small.
Largest known feathered dinosaur at 9 metres. A mid-sized tyrannosaur from China.
Apex predators of the Late Cretaceous.
Slightly smaller relative of T. rex from Alberta, Canada. Evidence of pack behaviour.
The 'tyrant lizard king'. One of the largest land predators ever.
Asian counterpart of T. rex. Apex predator of Late Cretaceous Mongolia.
Ostrich-like dinosaurs, likely omnivorous.
Fast-running ornithomimid from Mongolia.
Ostrich mimic from Late Cretaceous North America. One of the fastest dinosaurs.
Theropods with specialised grasping hands. Includes birds and their closest relatives.
Bizarre small theropods with powerful single-clawed arms, likely used for breaking into insect nests.
Small alvarezsaurid with a single large claw on each hand. Likely an insect specialist.
Bizarre pot-bellied theropods that became herbivores.
Bizarre theropod with enormous claws up to 1m long. Likely herbivorous.
Beaked theropods, many with elaborate crests. Known for nesting behaviour.
Misnamed 'egg thief' — likely brooding its own eggs when discovered.
Enormous oviraptorosaur weighing up to 2 tonnes. The largest known beaked dinosaur.
Dinosaurs close to the origin of flight. Many had feathered wings on both arms and legs.
The 'raptors' — agile predators with a sickle claw on each foot.
Four-winged raptor that could glide between trees. Had iridescent black feathers.
Small feathered predator from Mongolia. Turkey-sized, not as in Jurassic Park.
The raptor that inspired the Jurassic Park 'Velociraptors'. Sparked the dinosaur renaissance.
Largest known dromaeosaurid, up to 7 metres long.
Large raptor that lived alongside T. rex in the Hell Creek Formation.
Small, brainy theropods with large forward-facing eyes. Likely nocturnal hunters.
Had one of the largest brain-to-body ratios of any dinosaur.
Birds and their closest relatives — the surviving dinosaurs.
The 'first bird'. Transitional form between feathered dinosaurs and modern birds.
Early beaked bird from China. Males had long ribbon-like tail feathers.
Flightless toothed seabird from the Late Cretaceous. An expert diver.
Modern birds — the only surviving dinosaurs, with over 10,000 living species.
Long-necked, mostly herbivorous dinosaurs. Includes the largest land animals ever.
One of the first large herbivorous dinosaurs. Could walk on two or four legs.
Early sauropodomorph from southern Africa. Eggs and embryos have been found.
The giant long-necked dinosaurs. The largest land animals in Earth's history.
Middle Jurassic sauropod from China with a bony tail club — unusual for a sauropod.
Long, whip-tailed sauropods of the Late Jurassic.
Iconic long-necked dinosaur with a whip-like tail.
Formerly known as 'Brontosaurus'. Massive sauropod from North America.
Close relative of Diplodocus with an exceptionally long neck, possibly up to 9 metres.
Unusual sauropod from Argentina with two parallel rows of tall spines along its neck.
Most common sauropod of the Morrison Formation. Robust build with a boxy skull.
Tall sauropod with longer forelimbs than hindlimbs. Could reach 13m high.
African relative of Brachiosaurus, formerly classified as B. brancai. One of the tallest dinosaurs.
Unusual sauropod with a wide, straight-edged mouth containing over 500 teeth, like a living lawnmower.
The last and largest group of sauropods. Dominated the Cretaceous on all continents.
Armoured titanosaur from Argentina. First sauropod discovered with bony osteoderms.
One of the largest known land animals ever, estimated at 70-80 tonnes.
Contender for largest dinosaur ever. Estimated at 69 tonnes.
Massive titanosaur from Patagonia. One of the most complete giant sauropod skeletons known.
Bird-hipped dinosaurs. Despite the name, not ancestors of birds.
One of the earliest ornithischians. Small, bipedal herbivore from southern Africa.
Small early ornithischians with unusual differentiated teeth, including canine-like tusks.
Small herbivore with three distinct tooth types — unique among dinosaurs.
Armoured dinosaurs: stegosaurs and ankylosaurs.
One of the earliest armoured dinosaurs. Small and bipedal with hundreds of bony scutes.
Plated dinosaurs with rows of bony plates and tail spikes.
Iconic plated dinosaur with a spiked tail ('thagomizer').
African stegosaur with a mix of plates and long spikes along its back and tail.
Heavily armoured dinosaurs, some with tail clubs.
Armoured dinosaurs without tail clubs, relying on body armour for defence.
Spectacularly preserved nodosaur 'mummy' from Alberta. Even its skin colour (reddish-brown) was determined.
Tank-like armoured dinosaurs with massive bony tail clubs.
Heavily armoured dinosaur with a massive tail club.
Well-armoured ankylosaur with bony eyelids and a heavy tail club.
Advanced ornithischians including ornithopods and marginocephalians.
Bipedal/quadrupedal herbivores, including the duck-billed dinosaurs.
Small, swift bipedal herbivore from England. Once mistakenly thought to have lived in trees.
Large ornithopods that became the dominant herbivores of the Cretaceous.
Second dinosaur ever named. Had distinctive thumb spikes.
Iguanodont from Saharan Africa with a tall sail or hump on its back.
Duck-billed dinosaurs. Among the most successful herbivores of the Late Cretaceous.
Known for its long curved cranial crest, possibly used for vocalisation.
Duck-billed dinosaur with a helmet-shaped crest. Skin impressions show a pebbly texture.
Crested hadrosaur from Alberta with a distinctive hatchet-shaped crest.
One of the last non-avian dinosaurs. Large herds roamed North America.
The 'good mother lizard'. First dinosaur shown to care for its young in nesting colonies.
Dinosaurs with bony shelves or frills at the back of the skull.
Dome-headed dinosaurs, possibly used for flank-butting or display.
Dome-headed dinosaur, possibly used its thick skull for head-butting.
Horned pachycephalosaur — possibly a juvenile Pachycephalosaurus.
Horned and frilled dinosaurs.
Small, early ceratopsian. One of the most species-rich dinosaur genera.
Sheep-sized ceratopsian from Mongolia. Famous specimen found locked in combat with a Velociraptor.
Large horned dinosaurs of the Late Cretaceous.
Single-horned ceratopsian found in massive bonebeds suggesting herds of thousands.
Spectacular frill with long horn-like spikes.
Ceratopsian with an enormous heart-shaped frill, likely used for display.
Three-horned face. One of the last and most recognisable dinosaurs.
Had one of the largest skulls of any land animal at over 2.7m long. May be a mature Triceratops.
